3 min read
Just now

--

I got into an issue while learning pandas that what’s the real difference between loc and iloc in pandas. But after experimenting I found it. It’s really simple.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Just for the reference.Now most people say that the difference between those two would be that iloc is more of an index based access while loc is more label or Boolean based accessing.
It’s true, but under the hood its different.
Even though the above statement is true, iloc use something called standard library indexing scheme. You may wonder what’s that.
First of all Standard Library is a library which contains all the core utilities like built-in scripts, modules written in C which is highly used in python programming.
Examples:

• os

• math

• json
Now what is this indexing scheme? To demonstrate this I will provide an example of this.
list_1 = [1, 2, 3, 4]print(list_1[0]) # 1
print(list_1[-1]) # 4
print(list_1[0:4]) # [1, 2, 3, 4]
In here we access the first element via zero based indexing. So 1st element is the zero in index [zero indexing]. Notice that last element is accessed like this formula
Last Element's Index = Total Length - 1What I want to say here is that the end is always exclusive. Meaning that you get the index 1 and 2 in here but not 3.
This is exactly how iloc works. If you expect to return first 1000 entries out of 1500 data records, we have to write something like this using zero as the index.
nlist_df.iloc[0:1000] # 0, 1, 2 ..... 999# list_df.iloc[1:1000] # 0, 1, 2 ..... 999 : If we use 1 as the index
which of course would return the first 1000 entries. Now you may wonder, wouldn’t this return only 999 elements, wouldn’t this stop at the 999th index. But here’s the catch, as I said earlier the first element sits at the index zero so obviously it returns all the elements up to 1000.
But this is totally different in loc. It doesn’t follow this stdlib indexing scheme. If you were to write the same, it would only return the first 1001 elements because loc includes the end label as well
list_df.loc[0:1000]Why is that?
Because it works with labels not indexes. Labels are the index values you see in the left side of a Data Frame.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Geeks for GeeksSo what we do in loc is just access the elements based on labels which are defined in the table.
In conclusion.
We use iloc to access element based on index values (integers) where it follows the stdlib indexing scheme. While loc just use labels to access the elements.
This is not to make this concept more complex but to understand why these two are actually different from each other and how they actually work.
Yasas Banu.
